{{Short description|Argentine-American cognitive scientist}}
{{Use mdy dates|cs1-dates=ly|date=April 2025}}
{{Use list-defined references|date=April 2025}}
{{CS1 config|mode=cs2}}
'''Roxana Arleen Moreno''' (1960–2010) was an Argentine-American [[cognitive science|cognitive scientist]] and [[educational psychology|educational psychologist]], known for her work on [[cognitive load]] and [[multimedia learning]].

==Education and career==
Moreno was born in [[Detroit]], on October 25, 1960,{{r|diss}} to a family of Argentine immigrants who returned to Argentina when she was six.{{r|abio}}

After a 1984 bachelor's degree in economics at the [[University of Buenos Aires]],{{r|remembering|abio|diss}} Moreno received a [[juris doctor]] from the [[University of Buenos Aires]] in 1985.{{r|unm|diss}} She held various teaching positions in Argentina from 1985 to 1990.{{r|diss}}

By 1995 she had moved to California, and passed the [[State Bar of California|state bar]].{{r|diss}} She began studying psychology at the [[University of California, Santa Barbara]], received a master's degree in 1998,{{r|diss}} and completed her Ph.D. in 1999.{{r|diss|x}} Her dissertation, ''Introducing Social Cues in Multimedia Learning: The Role of Pedagogic Agents' Image and Language in a Scientific Lesson'', was supervised by [[Richard E. Mayer]].{{r|alumni|diss}}

After postdoctoral research funded by a [[National Science Foundation]] postdoctoral fellowship, she joined the educational psychology program at the [[University of New Mexico]] in 2000.{{r|unm}} She became an associate professor there{{r|x}} before dying of a long illness on July 24, 2010.{{r|unm}}

==Recognition==
''[[Contemporary Educational Psychology]]'' recognized Moreno as "one of the top 20 most productive educational psychologists in the world for 1991–2002".{{r|unm}} She was a 2003 recipient of the [[Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers]], given for "being an innovative researcher on how science teachers learn to apply principles of educational psychology to their classroom experiences through new technology tools and materials".{{r|pecase}}

The [[American Psychological Association]] gave her their Richard E. Snow Award for Early Contributions.{{r|unm}} The [[American Educational Research Association]] named her as a fellow in 2010.{{r|aera}}

She was a [[University of New Mexico]] Regent's Lecturer two times.{{r|unm}}
